purpose: This policy describes how <<LAB_NAME>> issues blood components in life-threatening emergencies when there is insufficient time for a full type, antibody screen, and crossmatch. CLIA at 42 CFR 493.1271(d) allows emergency release as defined in writing by the laboratory; FDA 21 CFR 606.151 requires written procedures for emergency release.

cfr_text_blocks.label: Standard: Compatibility testing (emergency exception)
cfr_text_blocks.verbatim: The laboratory must perform compatibility testing prior to the issuance of blood and blood products, except as provided for an emergency situation defined in writing by the laboratory.

cfr_text_blocks.label: FDA: Compatibility testing
cfr_text_blocks.verbatim: Standard operating procedures for compatibility testing shall include the following: (a) A method of collecting and identifying the blood samples of recipients to ensure positive identification. (b) The use of fresh recipient serum or plasma samples less than 3 days old for all pretransfusion testing if the recipient has been pregnant within the past 3 months or transfused within the past 3 months, or if the history is uncertain or unavailable. (c) Testing the recipient's serum or plasma with the donor's cells (major crossmatch) by a method that will demonstrate agglutinating, coating and hemolytic antibodies. (d) A procedure to expedite transfusion in life-threatening emergencies. Records of all such incidents shall be maintained, including complete documentation justifying the emergency action, which shall be signed by a physician.

scope: All <<LAB_NAME>> transfusion service personnel, ordering clinicians, and the medical director or designee.
policy_statements: Emergency release is invoked only by a physician who certifies in writing that the urgency of the patient's condition does not permit completion of pretransfusion testing. The physician's signature and the certification language are required at the time of release or as soon thereafter as practical.

Group O red cells are issued by default (Group O Rh-negative for women of childbearing potential, Group O Rh-positive otherwise) unless a current ABO/Rh result is available on the patient at <<LAB_NAME>>.

Pretransfusion testing (ABO/Rh, antibody screen, crossmatch) is completed as soon as feasible on the patient specimen drawn before or concurrent with the emergency release; any incompatibility is reported to the ordering physician immediately.

Every emergency release is documented in the transfusion service record with: patient identifier, components issued (unit number, ABO/Rh), date/time of issue, physician name and signature on the emergency certification, technologist, and any subsequent pretransfusion test results.
All emergency release events are reviewed by the medical director or designee within 5 business days; the review is signed and documented.
Emergency release records are retained for at least 10 years per 42 CFR 493.1105(a)(7).
procedure_steps: Ordering physician requests emergency release and provides certification (verbal at time of request, written within the same shift). The certification states that delay for full pretransfusion testing would risk patient life.
Transfusion service issues Group O red cells (Rh-negative for women of childbearing potential, Rh-positive otherwise) unless a current ABO/Rh on file at <<LAB_NAME>> supports type-specific issue.
The technologist documents the issue: patient ID, unit numbers issued, date/time, ordering physician, certification status, technologist initials.

The patient specimen drawn before or concurrent with the emergency release is processed for full pretransfusion testing as soon as the emergency is contained; results are reported promptly to the ordering physician.
If any incompatibility is detected on subsequent testing, the ordering physician is notified immediately.
The medical director or designee reviews the event within 5 business days and signs the review.
definitions: Issue of blood components without full pretransfusion testing under a physician certification that the urgency of the patient's condition does not permit delay.
Written statement signed by the ordering physician justifying the emergency action. Required by FDA 21 CFR 606.151(d).
